content.text: Why an AI Ethics Board Is Different from a Risk Committee

Most enterprises have risk committees and compliance functions. An AI Ethics Board is distinct in three ways: it operates prospectively (reviewing use cases before deployment, not just auditing incidents after the fact); it includes perspectives — philosophy, civil society, affected communities — that do not typically sit on risk committees; and its mandate explicitly covers harms that may be legal but ethically problematic.

For Claude deployments, an Ethics Board is most valuable when the organisation moves beyond productivity tools into use cases that affect customers, employees, or third parties: automated communication, AI-augmented hiring, credit and underwriting support, healthcare information, or any system where Claude outputs influence consequential decisions.

attrs.alt: AI Ethics Board governance structure showing review flows and decision outputs

content.text: Charter Fundamentals

A board without a clear charter is an advisory conversation, not a governance mechanism. The charter establishes the board's authority to approve, reject, or impose conditions on AI use cases — and the organisation's obligation to obtain that approval before deployment.
Core Charter Elements
Charter Section
Key Decisions to Make
Common Mistake
Mandate
Advisory vs. decision-making authority; scope (all AI, only high-risk, only Claude)
Advisory-only boards are ignored under deadline pressure — give binding authority for high-risk use cases
Membership
Internal vs. external members; quorum requirement; rotation policy
All-internal boards create conflict of interest — include at least one independent external ethicist or civil society representative
Meeting cadence
Regular quarterly session + triggers for extraordinary sessions
Quarterly-only boards create 90-day approval delays; define a fast-track path for lower-risk reviews
Review scope
Which use cases require full review vs. self-certification vs. no review
Undefined scope means everything or nothing goes to the board — define tiers explicitly
Decision types
Approve / reject / conditional approval / defer / escalate to board of directors
Binary approve/reject misses conditional approval, which is the most common outcome for useful but imperfect proposals
Accountability
Who the board reports to; how decisions are recorded; how they can be appealed
No appeal process creates perception that the board is a rubber stamp or an obstacle, not a governance mechanism
Generating a Charter with Claude

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_ai_ethics_board_charter(
    organisation_name: str,
    industry: str,
    primary_ai_uses: list[str],
    regulatory_context: list[str]
) -> dict:
    """Generate a draft AI Ethics Board charter using Claude."""
    uses_text = "\n".join(f"- {u}" for u in primary_ai_uses)
    regs_text = "\n".join(f"- {r}" for r in regulatory_context)

    prompt = f"""Draft a concise, practical AI Ethics Board charter for an enterprise organisation.

Organisation: {organisation_name}
Industry: {industry}

Primary AI uses to govern:
{uses_text}

Applicable regulatory frameworks:
{regs_text}

The charter must cover:
1. Purpose and mandate (why the board exists, what authority it has)
2. Membership composition (roles required, quorum, independence requirements)
3. Meeting cadence (regular sessions + triggers for extraordinary sessions)
4. Review scope (what the board must review vs. what is delegated)
5. Decision types (approve / reject / conditional / escalate)
6. Accountability and reporting (who the board reports to)
7. Charter review cycle

Write in plain English suitable for a board-level document. Practical and specific,
not aspirational boilerplate. Approximately 500 words.

Return as JSON with keys: purpose, membership, meeting_cadence, review_scope,
decision_types, accountability, review_cycle, and full_charter_text (the full draft)."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1200,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

charter = generate_ai_ethics_board_charter(
    organisation_name="Meridian Financial Group",
    industry="Financial services (banking, wealth management, insurance)",
    primary_ai_uses=[
        "Customer-facing chatbot for account queries",
        "Credit risk scoring augmentation",
        "Internal document processing and summarisation",
        "Employee productivity tools (Claude via enterprise gateway)"
    ],
    regulatory_context=[
        "EU AI Act (high-risk AI provisions for credit scoring)",
        "FCA Consumer Duty",
        "GDPR and UK GDPR",
        "PRA model risk management guidelines (SS1/23)"
    ]
)
print(f"Purpose: {charter['purpose']}")
print(f"\nMembership: {charter['membership']}")
print(f"\nMeeting cadence: {charter['meeting_cadence']}")
print(f"\nReview cycle: {charter['review_cycle']}")
Defining Review Scope

The most operationally consequential charter decision is which use cases require Ethics Board review. Too broad a scope creates a bottleneck; too narrow a scope leaves high-risk deployments unreviewed. A risk-tiered approach solves this:
Tier
Review Requirement
Turnaround
Example Use Cases
Tier 0 — Self-service
No review — self-certification against the approved use-case list
Immediate
Employee productivity tools, drafting assistance, internal summarisation
Tier 1 — Self-certification
Requestor completes a risk checklist; Ethics Board notified but does not convene
3 business days
Customer-facing responses with human review, low-stakes automation
Tier 2 — Board review (fast track)
Full Ethics Board review; can be convened within 5 business days
5–10 business days
Automated communications, HR tools, use cases involving personal data
Tier 3 — Board review + legal
Ethics Board review plus legal opinion; board of directors notification
15–20 business days
Credit decisions, regulatory filings, use cases involving protected characteristics
Use Case Risk Assessment
Automated risk scoring helps the Ethics Board triage incoming requests and assign them to the correct tier before convening a full session:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def assess_use_case_for_ethics_review(
    use_case_title: str,
    description: str,
    affected_stakeholders: list[str],
    data_involved: list[str],
    decision_autonomy: str
) -> dict:
    """Assess whether a proposed AI use case requires Ethics Board review."""
    stakeholders_text = ", ".join(affected_stakeholders)
    data_text = ", ".join(data_involved)
    prompt = f"""Assess whether this proposed Claude use case requires AI Ethics Board review.

Use case: {use_case_title}
Description: {description}
Affected stakeholders: {stakeholders_text}
Data types involved: {data_text}
Decision autonomy level: {decision_autonomy}
  (Options: "informational" — AI informs human; "augmented" — AI recommends, human approves;
   "automated" — AI decides without human review for each instance)

Score across these risk dimensions (1=low, 5=high):
- Potential for harm to individuals
- Data sensitivity
- Irreversibility of decisions
- Regulatory exposure
- Reputational risk if misused
- Scale of impact (number of people affected)

Return JSON with:
- dimension_scores: {{dimension_name: score, rationale: str}} for each dimension
- overall_risk_score: weighted average (harm and regulatory exposure weighted 1.5x)
- review_requirement: "no_review_needed" | "self_certification" | "ethics_board_review" | "ethics_board_plus_legal"
- review_rationale: 2 sentences explaining the decision
- conditions_for_lower_tier: what safeguards would reduce this to a lower review requirement
- fast_track_eligible: boolean (can this be reviewed in 5 business days vs. standard 15?)"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=700,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

assessment = assess_use_case_for_ethics_review(
    use_case_title="Automated Loan Decline Letter Generation",
    description="Claude generates personalised decline letters for mortgage applications that did not meet underwriting criteria. Letters are reviewed by a human underwriter before sending.",
    affected_stakeholders=["Mortgage applicants (retail customers)", "Underwriting team", "Compliance"],
    data_involved=["Customer name and address", "Loan application details", "Credit decision rationale (not raw score)"],
    decision_autonomy="augmented"
)
print(f"Review requirement: {assessment['review_requirement']}")
print(f"Overall risk score: {assessment['overall_risk_score']}/5")
print(f"Rationale: {assessment['review_rationale']}")
print(f"Fast track: {assessment['fast_track_eligible']}")
if assessment.get("conditions_for_lower_tier"):
    print(f"Lower tier conditions: {assessment['conditions_for_lower_tier']}")
Meeting Structure and Minutes

Ethics Board effectiveness depends on consistent meeting structure. A standard agenda for quarterly sessions: (1) minutes of previous meeting, (2) use case reviews, (3) policy updates, (4) incident and near-miss review, (5) regulatory horizon scan, (6) AOB. Claude can generate formal minutes from raw discussion notes:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_ethics_board_meeting_minutes(
    meeting_date: str,
    attendees: list[str],
    agenda_items: list[dict],
    raw_discussion_notes: str
) -> str:
    """Generate structured meeting minutes from raw discussion notes."""
    attendees_text = ", ".join(attendees)
    agenda_text = "\n".join(
        f"{i+1}. {item['title']}: {item['type']}"
        for i, item in enumerate(agenda_items)
    )
    prompt = f"""Draft formal AI Ethics Board meeting minutes from raw notes.

Meeting date: {meeting_date}
Attendees: {attendees_text}

Agenda:
{agenda_text}

Raw discussion notes:
---
{raw_discussion_notes}
---

Draft formal minutes that:
1. Summarise each agenda item with: context, key points raised, decision reached, action items
2. Record all formal decisions with explicit vote counts where applicable
3. Note any dissenting views on formal decisions
4. List all action items with owner and due date
5. Identify any items deferred to the next meeting

Format as professional board minutes. Be precise about decisions — never paraphrase
a decision in a way that could be misinterpreted later. If the notes are ambiguous
about a decision, note that it requires confirmation at the next meeting."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

minutes = generate_ethics_board_meeting_minutes(
    meeting_date="2025-06-15",
    attendees=["Sarah Chen (Chair, Chief Legal Officer)", "Marcus Webb (Chief Risk Officer)",
                "Dr. Priya Sharma (External Ethics Advisor)", "James Park (CISO)",
                "Olivia Reeves (HR Director)", "Tom Kimura (Head of AI Engineering)"],
    agenda_items=[
        {"title": "Automated loan decline letter use case", "type": "review_decision"},
        {"title": "EU AI Act compliance gap assessment", "type": "information"},
        {"title": "Q3 audit programme approval", "type": "approval"},
    ],
    raw_discussion_notes="""
    Loan decline letters: Priya raised concern about explainability — are the decline reasons
    accurate and complete? Tom confirmed letters pull directly from the underwriting decision
    record. James noted the human review step satisfies the FCA requirement. Vote: approved
    with condition that first 500 letters are audited by compliance before scaling.
    Marcus abstained pending legal opinion on Consumer Duty obligations.

    EU AI Act: Tom presented the gap assessment. Credit scoring system likely classified as
    high-risk. Legal review needed urgently. Sarah to commission external counsel review by end July.

    Q3 audit: Presented by Sarah. Covers 4 use cases deployed in Q1-Q2. All agreed.
    Olivia noted HR tool should be prioritised given sensitivity. Tom to schedule technical
    review week of July 14. Full board review of findings scheduled September 8.
    """
)
print(minutes[:800])
Membership Composition
Chief Legal Officer or General Counsel
— provides legal authority and regulatory awareness; often chairs the board
Chief Risk Officer
— enterprise risk perspective; connects AI risk to the organisation's overall risk appetite
Chief Information Security Officer
— technical security and data protection; essential for any use case involving sensitive data
HR Director
— employee and workforce implications; particularly important for AI tools used in hiring, performance management, or workforce planning
Business sponsor
— rotating seat for the business leader sponsoring the use case under review; ensures the board understands the business case, not just the risk
AI/Engineering lead
— technical feasibility and implementation realities; keeps the board grounded in how the system actually works
External ethics advisor
— independent perspective; may be an academic ethicist, civil society representative, or member of a relevant affected community

The external ethics advisor is the most important non-obvious membership requirement. Internal members all have organisational incentives that can compromise objectivity. One external voice with no institutional stake in the outcome changes the dynamics of every conversation.
Common Charter Failures
Advisory authority only

— if the board can only recommend and business units can override, every high-pressure situation will see the board's judgment bypassed. Give the board binding authority over Tier 2 and Tier 3 use cases at minimum.
No fast-track path
— a single 15-business-day review process for every use case guarantees that teams route around the board. Build a 5-day fast track for genuinely lower-risk proposals.
Undefined 'AI' scope

— if the charter governs 'AI' without specifying what counts, every spreadsheet formula and spell-checker is theoretically in scope. Define the scope explicitly: 'large language models and generative AI systems used for external communications, consequential internal decisions, or processing of personal data'.
No sunset or escalation clause

— AI governance needs evolve rapidly. A charter with no review cycle will be obsolete within 18 months. Mandate an annual charter review and a trigger clause for extraordinary revision when a significant regulatory change occurs.
Summary

An AI Ethics Board charter establishes the authority, membership, scope, and process for governing Claude and broader AI deployments before harm occurs, not after. The most important charter decisions are: granting binding authority (not advisory only) over high-risk use cases; defining a risk-tiered review structure with a fast-track path for lower-risk proposals; including at least one independent external member; and building a clear decision taxonomy that distinguishes approval, conditional approval, rejection, and escalation. Claude can draft the charter itself, score incoming use cases for risk tier assignment, and generate formal meeting minutes from raw discussion notes — reducing governance overhead while maintaining rigour.
